content.text: Default prop values let you specify what a component should use when a caller omits a prop. They reduce the burden on callers — optional props can be left out and the component still behaves correctly — and document the component's sensible baseline behaviour directly in the code.

attrs.alt: Decision tree showing when a default prop value applies: a passed string/number/object uses the passed value; null uses null (default NOT applied); undefined (omitted prop) triggers the default

content.text: The Key Rule: Defaults Respond to undefined Only
React passes the props object as-is to the component. JavaScript's destructuring default syntax activates only when a value is exactly

The standard way to set defaults is inside the destructuring pattern with
Before destructuring defaults became standard, React provided a
static property on the component. You may encounter it in older codebases:

as of React 18.3 and will be removed in a future version. Use destructuring defaults in all new code.
A default should represent the most common or neutral configuration of the prop. Guidelines:
Features should be opt-in; off is the safer baseline.
Reduces boilerplate at every call site.
Empty string renders nothing; no default forces the caller to decide.
null lets JSX conditional hide elements; no-op avoids null checks.
Empty Array Default: A Performance Subtlety

dependency, a new reference breaks memoization every render.
The fix is to use a stable module-level constant as the default. This pattern is only necessary when the default value is an object or array used in a hook dependency array or as a memo input.
TypeScript sees a prop with a destructuring default as optional — the caller may omit it. You still mark it
in the interface, and TypeScript narrows the type inside the function to exclude
Any prop with a default can always be overridden. Simply pass the desired value:

For form inputs, React distinguishes between
(DOM manages the value). Default prop values relate to this: the
sets the initial value for an uncontrolled input, while the
When building a component wrapper around an input, your default prop for the
(controlled) has different semantics. Mixing them causes the React warning:
"A component is changing an uncontrolled input to be controlled."

Compound components (where parent and child share implicit state via Context) use defaults to set the initial context value, which acts as the baseline for all children that do not receive an explicit override:
Expecting null to trigger the default
— it does not. If you must treat
the same as "missing", apply the default explicitly:
Using a mutable object as a default
creates a new object each render, which can break memoization. Use a module-level constant.
No default for a required prop
— a required prop with no default will be
if forgotten. TypeScript and PropTypes both catch this at development time; without them, you get silent rendering bugs.
When you spread props onto a child component, defaults in the child's signature still apply normally — but the ordering matters when you want to allow callers to override a spread default.
collects every prop that isn't explicitly named, so it never interferes with defaults. A common mistake is spreading
before an explicit value, which can accidentally overwrite the default:
null Coalescing and Logical OR vs Destructuring Defaults
JavaScript gives you three ways to supply a fallback: destructuring defaults, the nullish coalescing operator
. They differ in what they treat as "missing".
Destructuring defaults are usually the right choice inside component signatures because they handle
inside the function body when you want null to also fall back, and avoid
when the prop could legitimately be zero or an empty string (e.g. a count, a badge label).
Good tests verify both the default-applied path and the override path, and separately check the null-does-not-trigger-default behaviour.
Testing the null case explicitly prevents subtle regressions where a refactor accidentally changes the fallback logic from destructuring defaults to
(which would cause the test to fail, alerting you to the behaviour change).
Compound components — a parent that co-ordinates several child sub-components — benefit from defaults at each piece, reducing the amount the caller must specify.
Each sub-component carries its own defaults independently. This makes the public API ergonomic: callers only need to spell out props that deviate from the design system baseline.
Defaults and Controlled vs Uncontrolled Inputs
React distinguishes between controlled inputs (value driven by state) and uncontrolled inputs (value managed by the DOM). Default prop values interact differently with each pattern.
For controlled inputs, the value-carrying prop (typically

have a destructuring default: giving it a fallback would silently convert the input from controlled to uncontrolled (or vice-versa) when the caller forgets the prop. Make the requirement explicit by omitting the default.
When a default value is an object or array, placing it inline in the destructuring creates a
. For most components this is harmless, but it breaks
dependency arrays because the reference is always different.
This pattern—a module-level constant for stable object/array defaults—is the idiomatic solution in React codebases that lean on memoisation. If the component does not use
and the default is only used to avoid crashing on
calls, the inline default is fine.
A default value communicates intent: it says "when this prop is omitted, this is the most common and least surprising behaviour". Bad defaults cause silent bugs.
If your buttons are secondary by default in the design system, set
so the component matches the spec with no props.
so callers who don't need them don't have to pass anything, and the component never crashes on an optional callback call.
Avoid defaulting to a derived or computed value inline.
If the default depends on another prop, compute it inside the function body, not in the destructuring:
Most boolean props in React are opt-in flags (e.g.
to use the component normally — an awkward API.
No default for truly required props.
If a prop is essential (e.g. a database record
), omit the default and add a TypeScript required type or PropTypes check so the missing prop is caught at development time.
The following component brings together every default-prop technique covered in this section: destructuring defaults, stable references, optional callbacks, and null awareness.

to mean "no title", and the component checks
(so a title of "0" would still render).
uses a module-level constant for referential stability — safe to use in dependency arrays.
